He is tiny, so he doesn't often walk on his own — he'll normally be on someone else's shoulder or use their space to move. He is also a summoner with the Eidolon Contact background. His gimmick is that he was once a full-fledged adult forest dragon who pissed off one of the primary dragon gods, Apsu, enough that Apsu cursed him into dragonet form. Solanum would be on a ship known as the Whispering Word, a merchant ship that goes off the coast, does some fishing, general coastal runs. This would be one of those days after a storm when Solanum and the rest of the crew are back to port, checking whether everyone and the ship are okay and what they can do to get more supplies. One of the port runners would recognize Solanum and say, "Hey, you've got a letter," and give it to her. Her little rooty vines tangle through the letter, ripping little holes and smudging it, of course. But she gets remarkably excited about the opportunity to learn something new. She makes her way down to the kitchen where her own little home is — an old molded potato sack where the plant originally grew. She would probably want to tell her father figure, a catfolk by the name of Archibald Candlethaw: picture a really big burly cat in an apron a little too small, his guts hanging over it a bit. He sees the letter and equally says, "Well, what does it say?" And she says she was accepted — one of those "I'm so proud of you; my little plant gets to explore the world some more" moments. They decide to celebrate on the ship for the night.

He was stumbled upon by a druid teacher during a field trip and asked for help breaking his curse — the school has access to materials and knowledge he doesn't. They have a friendly, bantery dynamic. He's curious how other mortals live when they're not singularly focused on hoarding, and he wants to learn how to circumvent the curse so he can use his full strength again and do what he wants.

changes.new: Each of your packages contains a small ceramic bead along with your letter. As you begin to arrive on the docks of Nantambu, wherever you're carrying them, you feel a soft warmth and maybe a touch of vibration. As you look at them, one of the points on the star begins to glow. If you turn and rotate the bead, the glow shifts — always pointing in one direction.
Oh, that's sick. It's a compass — sort of.

You all follow your compasses. They slowly lead you through the main streets of Nantambu — a beautiful, bustling, modern city-state, mosaics everywhere, ringed with canals between each set of rings. At the heart of the city your compasses lead you to the massive campus of the Magaambya School of Magic: incredibly tall towering buildings, and because they teach a great deal of druidic practice, trees grown to ridiculous sizes with rope bridges running through them and whole buildings inside the trunks. The beads eventually lead you to the very center of the center island the Magaambya occupies. In the center of that garden is a huge arched gazebo-like structure on pillars, each pillar a beautiful sculpture representing a kind of magic — air, earth, force, fire, and more — and a huge mosaic on the floor that is a map of the entire surrounding region. Those of you who investigate the map, or who have a very high Perception, might notice that it slowly shifts as if updating to represent the actual state of the surrounding regions.

Takulu Ot stands before you once more and says, "At the Magaambya, we seek to understand our differences. Where others might dismiss a trait as irrelevant or troublesome, we understand that the things that make us unique in life are key to who we are as people. And sometimes it is who we are that defines our magic. Sometimes it is things about us—things that societies have taught us to hate—that show our talent and what it will be. To the north, people say that children with white or red hair have a strong potential for magic. Here in Nantambu, they recognize that left-handed people are skilled at undoing spells. And one student of mine is terrified of butterflies, even though he considers it ridiculous. In exchange for his fear of fluttering whimsy, he has one of the most orderly minds for games and strategy that I have ever seen. Sometimes we do not understand our magic, and that lack of understanding causes pain. People who are uncomfortable in their own skin are incredible shapechangers, but often feel unsettled until they recognize the shape they were meant to be. Those who feel an urge to jump from high places often become frightened of themselves, but are simply channeling an untrained will to fly. Sometimes our talents are not revealed by happy means. One student here had a family who planned her life out from birth to death, punishing her when she deviated the slightest bit. She only found freedom in the thought of her own grave, and as a result the dead began to speak to her. Here she has freedom to choose her own path, but her necromancy remains strong. You don't have to share, but I want you to ask yourselves: what is it about you that defines you, even if others rarely notice it? Is there something inside of you that pulls you, perhaps defying rational explanation? And with that in mind, I return to my first question: who are you, and how can that person best be guided to grow?"

She takes you by the dining hall, a large facility warmed by the fires of the kitchen in the back. changes.new: Esi leads you from the dining hall to your next location: the student supply store, affectionately called the Powderpile by most of the students, and introduces you to Xhokan, a male kobold inventory manager.

He will give you any supplies you need for introductory classes free of charge. He can also give you personal supplies, which he will charge for — but you will not find better prices, especially with a student discount. He has most common things. He explicitly does not sell anything intentionally dangerous — you will not find magic weapons here, or rare reagents for destructive spells — but that's mostly a matter of trust and liability. Certain faculty and students who have proven themselves capable of handling such things may be provided those as necessary for research and education. He is also who you see about your disbursements.

You… I see confusion. Most colleges charge you a great deal to go there. Magaambya pays you. You will each be given a stipend of 40 gold today as you're starting, and monthly you will receive an additional 4 gold to cover living expenses and various needs you might have off campus.
As much as you like. The campus dining hall doesn't charge anything. There's no cost for the dormitories. All of the things you need for basic schooling are free.

Xhokan finally waddles out from behind a counter that was clearly not designed for a kobold-sized person — you can barely see him over it — with bags of gold pieces and a clipboard, and begins processing paperwork for all of you to receive your stipends.

And he provides you each with some basic supplies — parchment, quills, ink — and gives you a bag of 40 gold. He notably makes you sign a piece of paperwork for the 40 gold, and then sign an additional piece of paperwork for your monthly 4 gold. And if any of you dare to suggest that those should be one transaction and not two separate documented transactions, he will be quite upset.
Because one of those is a one-time payment and the other is recurring.
